title: Authors and Citation

title: Authors and Citation
text: Buzaladze G (2026). surveyorso: comprehensive toolkit survey research analytics. R package version 0.0.0.9000, https://giobuzala.github.io/surveyorso/.
code: @Manual{,   title = {surveyorso: A comprehensive toolkit for survey research and analytics},   author = {Giorgi Buzaladze},   year = {2026},   note = {R package version 0.0.0.9000},   url = {https://giobuzala.github.io/surveyorso/}, }

what: A comprehensive toolkit for survey research and analytics
title: A comprehensive toolkit for survey research and analytics
text: surveyorso R package providing unified toolkit end--end survey research workflows. designed applied survey analysts want fast, consistent, reproducible ways clean data, manage metadata, tabulate results, run advanced analytics, prepare outputs reporting, built-support SPSS-style metadata data types, variable labels, value levels.

title: A comprehensive toolkit for survey research and analytics
text: Install GitHub using remotes: Alternatively, install using pak:
code: install.packages("remotes") remotes::install_github("giobuzala/surveyorso") install.packages("pak") pak::pak("giobuzala/surveyorso")

title: A comprehensive toolkit for survey research and analytics
text: Single response questions: tab(), tab_all() Multiple response (pick-) questions: tab_mr(), tab_mr_all() Grid questions: tab_grid(), tab_grid_all(), tab_grid_t(), tab_grid_t_all()

title: A comprehensive toolkit for survey research and analytics
text: Merging stacking waves: merge_waves()

title: A comprehensive toolkit for survey research and analytics
text: Standardization normalization: standardize(), normalize() Feature engineering: one_hot_encode() Correlation tables: cor_matrix() Correlation heatmaps: cor_heatmap()

title: A comprehensive toolkit for survey research and analytics
text: K-means grid search: kmeans_grid()

title: A comprehensive toolkit for survey research and analytics
text: Straightlining detection: strline() Low-quality open-ended response detection (API-based): lowqual_gpt()

title: A comprehensive toolkit for survey research and analytics
text: package supports : Excel workbooks human coding (export_coding() → import_coding()), AI-assisted workflows generate themes assign codes (theme_gpt() → code_gpt()). use AI-assisted functions, set OpenAI API key current R session store local .Renviron file persistent use.
code: # Set your OpenAI API key in the current R session Sys.setenv(OPENAI_API_KEY="your_api_key")  # 1) Create a draft theme list theme_list <- theme_gpt(survey_data, x = Q5)  # 2a) Code responses and append binary-coded variables directly to the dataset survey_data_coded <- code_gpt(survey_data, x = Q5, theme_list = theme_list) %>%   code_gpt_to_df()  # 2b) Or write codes into an Excel coding workbook created by export_coding() for human review code_gpt(survey_data, x = Q5, theme_list = theme_list) %>%   code_gpt_to_excel(path = "Data")

title: A comprehensive toolkit for survey research and analytics
text: Combining variables: combine()

title: A comprehensive toolkit for survey research and analytics
text: functions documented examples using roxygen2. Browse help pages ?tab, ?standardize, ?strline, ?code_gpt.

what: Code open-ended survey responses using the OpenAI API — code_gpt
title: Code open-ended survey responses using the OpenAI API — code_gpt
text: function submits open-ended survey responses OpenAI API returns dataset original responses, assigned codes, bins provided code list. theme_list argument includes Description column, text incorporated additional coding instructions. Additional custom instructions can also supplied instructions argument.

title: Code open-ended survey responses using the OpenAI API — code_gpt
code: code_gpt(   data,   x,   theme_list,   id_var,   n = NULL,   batch_size = 100,   model = "gpt-4o",   instructions = NULL )

title: Code open-ended survey responses using the OpenAI API — code_gpt
text: data data frame containing survey data. x open-ended variable coded. theme_list data frame least two columns: Code Bin. id_var respondent ID variable. n Optional integer; number responses code. Defaults rows. Useful testing coding quality without coding every response. batch_size Integer; number responses per API call. Defaults 100. model Character string; OpenAI model use. Defaults gpt-4o. instructions Optional string; additional instructions coding.

title: Code open-ended survey responses using the OpenAI API — code_gpt
text: table respondent IDs, responses, codes, bins.

title: Code open-ended survey responses using the OpenAI API — code_gpt
text: Requires OpenAI API key, can generated https://platform.openai.com/, set R session using Sys.setenv(OPENAI_API_KEY="..."). Use code_gpt_to_df() update dataset results code_gpt_to_excel() export coded responses Excel.

title: Code open-ended survey responses using the OpenAI API — code_gpt
code: # Code Q5 Q5_coded <- code_gpt(data = survey_data, x = Q5, theme_list = theme_list, id_var = id_var, model = "gpt-4o") #> Error: These packages are required but not installed: dplyr, stringr

what: Process code_gpt() output to the dataset — code_gpt_to_df
title: Process code_gpt() output to the dataset — code_gpt_to_df
text: function takes code_gpt() result table merges binary Unchecked/Checked code variables back original survey dataset, preserving labels metadata.

title: Process code_gpt() output to the dataset — code_gpt_to_df
code: code_gpt_to_df(coded_tbl)

title: Process code_gpt() output to the dataset — code_gpt_to_df
text: coded_tbl dataset returned code_gpt() coding results.

title: Process code_gpt() output to the dataset — code_gpt_to_df
text: original dataset specified code_gpt() factor-coded variables (levels: Unchecked, Checked) appended code.

title: Process code_gpt() output to the dataset — code_gpt_to_df
text: Use function immediately code_gpt() pipeline, save result dataset. example: append binary coded_ variables code list.
code: data <- code_gpt(data = survey_data, x = Q5, theme_list = theme_list)    code_gpt_to_df()

title: Process code_gpt() output to the dataset — code_gpt_to_df
code: # Code Q5 and add to the dataset data <- code_gpt(data = survey_data, x = Q5, theme_list = theme_list) %>%   code_gpt_to_df() #> Error in code_gpt(data = survey_data, x = Q5, theme_list = theme_list) %>%     code_gpt_to_df(): could not find function "%>%"

what: Write code_gpt() results to Excel workbook — code_gpt_to_excel
title: Write code_gpt() results to Excel workbook — code_gpt_to_excel
text: function inserts coded responses (Code(s) column) code_gpt() result table corresponding Excel coding workbook created question.

title: Write code_gpt() results to Excel workbook — code_gpt_to_excel
code: code_gpt_to_excel(coded_tbl, path = "Data")

title: Write code_gpt() results to Excel workbook — code_gpt_to_excel
text: coded_tbl dataset returned code_gpt() coding results. path Directory containing coding workbook. Defaults "Data".

title: Write code_gpt() results to Excel workbook — code_gpt_to_excel
text: Excel workbook updated place.

title: Write code_gpt() results to Excel workbook — code_gpt_to_excel
text: function locates workbook named X Coding Workbook.xlsx, matches coded responses respondent ID, writes codes Code(s) coding sheet. import_coding() function can used import process coding workbook. Use function immediately code_gpt() pipeline write coded results directly corresponding Excel workbook. need save dataset, workbook updated place. example: update Q5 Coding Workbook place coded responses, can imported using import_coding().
code: code_gpt(data = survey_data, x = Q5, theme_list = theme_list)    code_gpt_to_excel()

title: Write code_gpt() results to Excel workbook — code_gpt_to_excel
code: # Update "Q5 Coding Workbook" in place with the coded responses code_gpt(data = survey_data, x = Q5, theme_list = theme_list) %>%   code_gpt_to_excel() #> Error in code_gpt(data = survey_data, x = Q5, theme_list = theme_list) %>%     code_gpt_to_excel(): could not find function "%>%"

what: Combine split variables into a single variable — combine
title: Combine split variables into a single variable — combine
text: function merges two variables represent split versions question (e.g., Q1_1 asked one group Q1_2 another) single combined variable. function fills missing values one variable non-missing values , preserving factor levels SPSS-style attributes.

title: Combine split variables into a single variable — combine
code: combine(data, var_1, var_2, new_name = NULL, new_label = NULL)

title: Combine split variables into a single variable — combine
text: data data frame containing variables combine. var_1 First variable merge. var_2 Second variable merge. new_name (Optional) Name new variable. omitted, defaults var_1_var_2_COMB. new_label (Optional) SPSS-style variable label. omitted, label var_1 copied.

title: Combine split variables into a single variable — combine
text: modified data frame new combined variable placed immediately var_2.

title: Combine split variables into a single variable — combine
text: variables valid values row, var_1 takes precedence (warning issued). Factor levels inherited var_1. Existing variables name overwritten. apply function across multiple pairs split variables, can map using code block :
code: pairs <- list(   list("Q1_1", "Q1_2"),   list("Q2_1", "Q2_2") )  dat <- reduce(   pairs,   function(d, vars) {     combine(d, !!sym(vars[[1]]), !!sym(vars[[2]]))   },   .init = dat )

title: Combine split variables into a single variable — combine
code: # Combine Q1_1 and Q2_2 data <- combine(data = survey_data, var_1 = Q1_1, var_2 = Q1_2) #> Error: These packages are required but not installed: dplyr

title: Plot a correlation heatmap — cor_heatmap
text: function creates interactive heatmap correlation matrix. Hover labels display variable pairs correlation values.

title: Plot a correlation heatmap — cor_heatmap
code: cor_heatmap(cor_mat, exclude = NULL)

title: Plot a correlation heatmap — cor_heatmap
text: cor_mat Correlation matrix data frame. exclude (Optional) Variable(s) exclude.

title: Plot a correlation heatmap — cor_heatmap
text: plotly heatmap object.

title: Plot a correlation heatmap — cor_heatmap
text: function accepts output cor_matrix() optionally allows excluding selected variables visualization.

title: Plot a correlation heatmap — cor_heatmap
code: # Basic heatmap pipeline cor_matrix(data = data) %>% cor_heatmap() #> Error in cor_matrix(data = data) %>% cor_heatmap(): could not find function "%>%"

title: Compute a correlation matrix — cor_matrix
text: function computes correlation matrix across numeric variables dataset.

title: Compute a correlation matrix — cor_matrix
code: cor_matrix(   data,   weight = NULL,   id_var,   method = "pearson",   round = 3,   half = TRUE,   min = NULL )

title: Compute a correlation matrix — cor_matrix
text: data data frame containing variables correlate. weight (Optional) Name weight variable. NULL, computes unweighted correlations. id_var ID column name exclude. method Correlation method (pearson", "spearman", "kendall"). round Number decimal places round correlations . half Logical; TRUE (default), displays lower triangle. FALSE, displays full matrix. min (Optional) Numeric threshold. specified, correlations |r| < min replaced NA.

title: Compute a correlation matrix — cor_matrix
text: correlation matrix, data frame.

title: Compute a correlation matrix — cor_matrix
text: numeric variables allowed. Non-numeric variables (e.g., factors, characters, dates) trigger error.

title: Compute a correlation matrix — cor_matrix
code: # Weighted Pearson (default) correlations, lower half of the matrix shown cor_matrix(data = data_clust, weight = weight_var) #> Error: These packages are required but not installed: wCorr  # Unweighted Spearman correlations, full matrix shown, with minimum threshold of 0.3 cor_matrix(data = data_clust, method = "spearman", half = FALSE, min = 0.3) #> Error: These packages are required but not installed: wCorr

title: Export a coding workbook — export_coding
text: function creates Excel-based coding workbook open-ended survey questions. export workbook single variable, set x = var. export multiple variables , provide vector, e.g., x = c(var1, var2, var3). Optionally, can include filter variable appear workbook easier sorting filtering.

title: Export a coding workbook — export_coding
code: export_coding(data, x, path = "Data", id_var, filter = NULL)

title: Export a coding workbook — export_coding
text: data data frame containing survey responses. Must include respondent ID (id_var) column variables specified x. x single variable multiple variables specifying open-ended question(s) export. path File path workbook(s) saved. Defaults "Data". id_var ID variable dataset. filter (Optional) single variable used group filter responses exported workbook.

title: Export a coding workbook — export_coding
text: One Excel workbooks saved specified path.

title: Export a coding workbook — export_coding
text: workbook includes: Coding Workbook sheet containing responses, along columns codes bins. Codes sheet pre-populated standard codes (97 = , 98 = None, 99 = know) space custom codes. workbook also includes Excel formulas bin lookups, counts, percentages, conditional formatting highlight invalid codes. NOTE: formula cell D2 first sheet must filled end column. Populating formula programmatically R causes Excel slow .

title: Export a coding workbook — export_coding
code: # Export coding workbook for Q5 export_coding(data = survey_data, x = Q5) #> Error: These packages are required but not installed: tidyselect, dplyr, openxlsx  # Export separate coding workbooks for Q5 and Q6 export_coding(data = survey_data, x = c(Q5, Q6)) #> Error: These packages are required but not installed: tidyselect, dplyr, openxlsx

what: Import a coding workbook and merge coded variable into the dataset — import_coding
title: Import a coding workbook and merge coded variable into the dataset — import_coding
text: function imports coded Excel workbook specific open-ended question merges processed coded variables back dataset.

title: Import a coding workbook and merge coded variable into the dataset — import_coding
code: import_coding(data, x, path = "Data", id_var)

title: Import a coding workbook and merge coded variable into the dataset — import_coding
text: data data frame containing least id_var original question column. x variable imported (e.g., Q5). path Directory containing workbook. Defaults "Data". id_var ID variable dataset.

title: Import a coding workbook and merge coded variable into the dataset — import_coding
text: data frame merged new coded columns. unknown codes detected, function prints table respondent IDs problematic codes stops error.

title: Import a coding workbook and merge coded variable into the dataset — import_coding
text: function handles one question time.

title: Import a coding workbook and merge coded variable into the dataset — import_coding
code: # Import coding workbook for Q5 and merge the results into the dataset data <- import_coding(data = survey_data, x = Q5, path = "Data", id_var = id_var) #> Error in import_coding(data = survey_data, x = Q5, path = "Data", id_var = id_var): could not find function "import_coding"

what: Run a grid of k-means clustering models — kmeans_grid
title: Run a grid of k-means clustering models — kmeans_grid
text: function tests multiple k-means clustering solutions, optionally applying principal component analysis (PCA) dimensionality reduction. Models always run raw standardized data (PCA). pc_range provided, additional models run using first N principal components. Results include cluster sizes, silhouette statistics, within-cluster sum squares (WSS). Use mean_sil total_wss identify optimal cluster configurations.

title: Run a grid of k-means clustering models — kmeans_grid
code: kmeans_grid(data, pc_range = NULL, k_range = 2:6, id_var = NULL)

title: Run a grid of k-means clustering models — kmeans_grid
text: data data frame numeric variables cluster. Non-numeric columns dropped. pc_range Optional numeric vector principal component counts. NULL, PCA applied. k_range Numeric vector cluster counts test. Defaults 2:6. id_var ID variable drop clustering.

title: Run a grid of k-means clustering models — kmeans_grid
text: data frame summarizing results combination PCs clusters.

title: Run a grid of k-means clustering models — kmeans_grid
text: function automatically excludes variable named "weight" clustering input.

title: Run a grid of k-means clustering models — kmeans_grid
code: # K-means runs (2-5 k-range) with and without PCA (2-4 PCs range) kmeans_results <- kmeans_grid(data = data_clust, pc_range = 2:4, k_range = 2:5) #> Error: These packages are required but not installed: dplyr

what: Identify low-quality open-ended responses using the OpenAI API — lowqual_gpt
title: Identify low-quality open-ended responses using the OpenAI API — lowqual_gpt
text: function flags low-quality open-ended survey responses using OpenAI API. Responses evaluated signs gibberish, nonsense, random text, irrelevant words, indicators poor data quality.

title: Identify low-quality open-ended responses using the OpenAI API — lowqual_gpt
code: lowqual_gpt(data, x, batch_size = 100, model = "gpt-4o")

title: Identify low-quality open-ended responses using the OpenAI API — lowqual_gpt
text: data data frame containing survey data. x open-ended variable evaluate response quality. batch_size Integer; number responses per API call. Defaults 100. model Character string; OpenAI model use. Defaults gpt-4o.

title: Identify low-quality open-ended responses using the OpenAI API — lowqual_gpt
text: data frame additional variable named x_lowqual containing classification results.

title: Identify low-quality open-ended responses using the OpenAI API — lowqual_gpt
text: Requires OpenAI API key, can generated https://platform.openai.com/, set R session using Sys.setenv(OPENAI_API_KEY="..."). model classifies response low-quality (1) valid (0). response flagged low-quality : Gibberish random characters -topic meaningless Contains emojis irrelevant text response considered valid interpretable, relevant, meaningful. function appends new column dataset results.

title: Identify low-quality open-ended responses using the OpenAI API — lowqual_gpt
code: # Identify low-quality respondents in question Q5 data <- lowqual_gpt(data = survey_data, x = Q5) #> Error: These packages are required but not installed: dplyr, stringr

title: Merge two survey waves — merge_waves
text: function merges new survey wave old one preserving variable labels allowing flexible control variables retained. provides two options variables retain: "New shared old" (default): keeps variables new dataset, plus shared variables old dataset, additional old-variables specified keep_old. "": keeps variables datasets. Variable labels reapplied merging: variables present new dataset, labels taken new_data. old-variables (included), labels taken old_data.

title: Merge two survey waves — merge_waves
code: merge_waves(   new_data,   old_data,   vars = c("New and shared old", "All"),   keep_old = NULL )

title: Merge two survey waves — merge_waves
text: new_data data frame new wave. old_data data frame old wave. vars Character string, either "New shared old" (default) "". keep_old (Optional) character vector old-variables keep vars = "New shared old".

title: Merge two survey waves — merge_waves
text: merged data frame harmonized variable labels.

title: Merge two survey waves — merge_waves
code: # Merge new data and shared variables from old data merged_data <- merge_waves(new_data = new_data, old_data = old_data, vars = "New and shared old") #> Error: These packages are required but not installed: dplyr

what: Normalize numeric and factor variables to a 0–1 scale — normalize
title: Normalize numeric and factor variables to a 0–1 scale — normalize
text: function rescales variables 0 1 range. supports flipping scales preserves variable labels.

title: Normalize numeric and factor variables to a 0–1 scale — normalize
code: normalize(   data,   vars = everything(),   flip = FALSE,   keep_all = TRUE,   outside_scale = c("Don't know", "Prefer not to say") )

title: Normalize numeric and factor variables to a 0–1 scale — normalize
text: data data frame containing variables normalize. vars Variables normalize. Supports tidyselect syntax (e.g., starts_with(), c(var1, var2)). flip Logical; TRUE, reverses direction valid response levels scaling. keep_all Logical; TRUE, returns full dataset; FALSE, returns normalized variables. outside_scale Character vector response levels (typically DK/PNTS) lie outside scale treated neutral midpoint (0.5).

title: Normalize numeric and factor variables to a 0–1 scale — normalize
text: data frame normalized variables. keep_all = TRUE, full dataset returned normalized variables. keep_all = FALSE, normalized variables returned.

title: Normalize numeric and factor variables to a 0–1 scale — normalize
text: Numeric variables min–max scaled 0 1. Factor variables converted numeric order evenly spaced 0 1. Levels listed outside_scale (e.g., know) assigned value 0.5.

title: Normalize numeric and factor variables to a 0–1 scale — normalize
code: # Normalize Q1 and Q2 with flipping the scale order normalize(data = data, vars = c(Q2, Q3), flip = TRUE) #> Error: These packages are required but not installed: tidyselect, dplyr

title: One-hot encode categorical variables — one_hot_encode
text: function converts one categorical variables data frame one-hot encoded variables.

title: One-hot encode categorical variables — one_hot_encode
code: one_hot_encode(data, vars, keep_all = TRUE)

title: One-hot encode categorical variables — one_hot_encode
text: data data frame containing variables encode. vars Variables one-hot encode. keep_all Logical; TRUE, returns full dataset; FALSE, returns one-hot encoded variables.

title: One-hot encode categorical variables — one_hot_encode
text: data frame one-hot encoded variables. keep_all = TRUE, full dataset returned one-hot encoded variables replacing originals. keep_all = FALSE, one-hot encoded variables returned.

title: One-hot encode categorical variables — one_hot_encode
text: Binary factors labeled Unchecked/Checked encoded single 0–1 variable. Multi-category factors expanded multiple one-hot encoded variables (one per level). Original variable labels preserved extended generated one-hot encoded variables.

title: One-hot encode categorical variables — one_hot_encode
code: # One-hot encode region and Q1_1 while keeping all original variables data <- one_hot_encode(data = data, c(region, Q1_1)) #> Error: These packages are required but not installed: tidyselect, dplyr, stringr  # One-hot encode region and Q1_1, returning only the encoded columns data_new <- one_hot_encode(data = data, c(region, Q1_1), keep_all = FALSE) #> Error: These packages are required but not installed: tidyselect, dplyr, stringr

what: Standardize numeric and factor variables (z-scores) — standardize
title: Standardize numeric and factor variables (z-scores) — standardize
text: function standardizes variables mean 0 standard deviation 1.

title: Standardize numeric and factor variables (z-scores) — standardize
code: standardize(   data,   vars = everything(),   flip = FALSE,   keep_all = TRUE,   outside_scale = c("Don't know", "Prefer not to say") )

title: Standardize numeric and factor variables (z-scores) — standardize
text: data data frame containing variables standardize. vars Variables standardize. Supports tidyselect syntax (e.g., starts_with(), c(var1, var2)). flip Logical; TRUE, reverses direction valid response levels standardization. keep_all Logical; TRUE, returns full dataset; FALSE, returns standardized variables. outside_scale Character vector response levels (typically DK/PNTS) lie outside scale treated neutral midpoint (0.5).

title: Standardize numeric and factor variables (z-scores) — standardize
text: data frame standardized variables. keep_all = TRUE, full dataset returned standardized variables. keep_all = FALSE, standardized variables returned.

title: Standardize numeric and factor variables (z-scores) — standardize
text: Numeric variables z-scored. Factor variables converted numeric order standardized. Levels outside_scale (e.g., know) assigned midpoint valid range transformation.

title: Standardize numeric and factor variables (z-scores) — standardize
code: # Standardize Q1 variable set with standardize(data = data, vars = starts_with("Q1_")) #> Error: These packages are required but not installed: tidyselect, dplyr  # Standardize Q1 and Q2 variable set with the scale order standardize(data = data, vars = c(Q1, Q2), flip = TRUE) #> Error: These packages are required but not installed: tidyselect, dplyr

what: Identify straightliners in grid questions — strline
title: Identify straightliners in grid questions — strline
text: function flags respondents selected response across items grid question.

title: Identify straightliners in grid questions — strline
code: strline(data, x)

title: Identify straightliners in grid questions — strline
text: data data frame containing survey data. x One variable prefixes identifying grid question(s).

title: Identify straightliners in grid questions — strline
text: original data frame additional logical column named prefix_strline, indicating whether respondent provided identical responses across items grid.

title: Identify straightliners in grid questions — strline
text: function automatically detects variables begin specified prefix followed underscore. excludes multiple response sets (e.g., binary 0/1 Unchecked/Checked grids).

title: Identify straightliners in grid questions — strline
code: # Identify straightliners for one grid question data <- strline(data = survey_data, x = Q1) #> Error: These packages are required but not installed: dplyr, stringr  # Identify straightliners for multiple grid questions data <- strline(data = survey_data, c(Q1, Q2)) #> Error: These packages are required but not installed: dplyr, stringr

what: Create a table or crosstab — tab
title: Create a table or crosstab — tab
text: function produces table (single variable) crosstab (two variables). x specified, output table x. x y specified, output crosstab x y. Use weight argument produce weighted tables; omitted, results unweighted. Set prop = TRUE (default) output proportions, prop = FALSE output frequencies. Use round argument control decimal precision proportions tables. numeric = TRUE, function removes total row coerces values numeric, making output math-ready (e.g., can multiplied added directly).

title: Create a table or crosstab — tab
code: tab(   data,   x,   y = NULL,   weight = NULL,   prop = TRUE,   total = TRUE,   round = 3,   numeric = FALSE )

title: Create a table or crosstab — tab
text: data data frame containing survey data. x variable compute table (row variable crosstab). y (Optional) variable cross-tabulate x. Defaults NULL. weight (Optional) numeric weighting variable. NULL (default), results unweighted. prop Logical; TRUE (default), outputs proportions FALSE, outputs frequencies. total Logical; TRUE (default), adds total column y specified. Ignored single-variable tables. round Integer; number decimal places proportions. Defaults 3. numeric Logical; TRUE, returns data frame numeric columns, n row removed. Defaults FALSE.

title: Create a table or crosstab — tab
text: table (y = NULL) crosstab (y specified), data frame.

title: Create a table or crosstab — tab
code: # Weighted proportions table of Q1 tab(data = survey_data, x = Q1, weight = weight_var) #> Error: These packages are required but not installed: dplyr, tidyr, stringr, crayon  # Weighted frequency crosstab of Q1 by region tab(data = survey_data, x = Q1, y = region, weight = weight_var, prop = FALSE) #> Error: These packages are required but not installed: dplyr, tidyr, stringr, crayon

what: Apply tab() to multiple variables — tab_all
title: Apply tab() to multiple variables — tab_all
text: function maps tab() across variables data frame. Optionally, can specify y variable crosstabs /exclude variables tabulated. Additional arguments passed tab().

title: Apply tab() to multiple variables — tab_all
code: tab_all(data, y = NULL, weight = NULL, exclude = NULL, ...)

title: Apply tab() to multiple variables — tab_all
text: data data frame containing survey data. y (Optional) variable cross-tabulate x variables. exclude Variables exclude tabulation. Defaults NULL. Always excludes y (specified), weight (specified), variable literally named "weight". ... Additional arguments passed tab().

title: Apply tab() to multiple variables — tab_all
text: named list tables crosstabs, one per variable.

title: Apply tab() to multiple variables — tab_all
code: # Unweighted proportions tables of all questions tab_all(data = survey_data) #> Error: These packages are required but not installed: dplyr  # Weighted proportions crosstabs of all questions by region, excluding Q99 tab_all(data = survey_data, y = region, weight = weight_var, exclude = "Q99") #> Error: These packages are required but not installed: dplyr

what: Create a full distribution table for a grid question — tab_grid
title: Create a full distribution table for a grid question — tab_grid
text: function produces table grid survey questions, showing full distribution across response levels. x specified, output full-distribution table item grid question. Use weight argument produce weighted tables; omitted, results unweighted. Set prop = TRUE (default) output proportions, prop = FALSE output frequencies. Use sort argument (asc desc) order rows based top/bottom box scores defined top_bottom. Set sort = NULL sorting. Use top_bottom argument (e.g., T2, B3) specify many top bottom levels use sorting grid items. Use round argument control decimal precision proportions tables. numeric = TRUE, function removes base column coerces values numeric, making output math-ready.

title: Create a full distribution table for a grid question — tab_grid
code: tab_grid(   data,   x,   weight = NULL,   prop = TRUE,   sort = NULL,   top_bottom = T2,   round = 3,   numeric = FALSE )

title: Create a full distribution table for a grid question — tab_grid
text: data data frame containing survey data. x variable prefix identifying grid question (e.g., Q3 Q3_1, Q3_2, ...). weight (Optional) numeric weighting variable. NULL (default), results unweighted. prop Logical; TRUE (default), outputs proportions. FALSE, outputs frequencies. sort Sorting order rows: desc, asc, NULL (sorting). Defaults NULL. top_bottom string form TN BN indicating many top bottom levels used sorting rows. round Integer; number decimal places proportions. Defaults 3. numeric Logical; TRUE, returns data frame numeric columns, n column removed. Defaults FALSE.

title: Create a full distribution table for a grid question — tab_grid
text: table showing full distribution across levels, data frame..

title: Create a full distribution table for a grid question — tab_grid
code: # Weighted proportions table of a grid question, sorted descending by T2 tab_grid(data = survey_data, x = Q3, weight = weight_var, sort = desc, top_bottom = T2) #> Error: These packages are required but not installed: dplyr, crayon

what: Create a top/bottom box table or crosstab for a grid question — tab_grid_t
title: Create a top/bottom box table or crosstab for a grid question — tab_grid_t
text: function produces top- bottom-box summary grid survey questions. x specified, output top- bottom-box table item grid question. x y specified, output crosstab selected top/bottom box y. Use top_bottom argument (e.g., T2, B3) define many top bottom response levels included box. Use weight argument produce weighted tables; omitted, results unweighted. Set prop = TRUE (default) output proportions, prop = FALSE output frequencies. Use total argument include total column y variable specified. Ignored one-way tables. Use sort argument (asc desc) order rows box score. Set sort = NULL sorting. Use round argument control decimal precision proportions tables. numeric = TRUE, function removes base row coerces values numeric, making output math-ready.

title: Create a top/bottom box table or crosstab for a grid question — tab_grid_t
code: tab_grid_t(   data,   x,   y = NULL,   top_bottom = T2,   weight = NULL,   prop = TRUE,   total = TRUE,   sort = NULL,   round = 3,   numeric = FALSE )

title: Create a top/bottom box table or crosstab for a grid question — tab_grid_t
text: data data frame containing survey data. x variable prefix identifying grid question (e.g., Q3 Q3_1, Q3_2, ...). y (Optional) variable name cross-tabulate grid question. Defaults NULL. top_bottom string form TN BN indicating many top bottom levels include box (e.g., T2 = top 2). Defaults T2. weight (Optional) numeric weighting variable. NULL (default), results unweighted. prop Logical; TRUE (default), outputs proportions. FALSE, outputs frequencies. total Logical; TRUE (default), adds total column y specified. sort Sorting order rows: desc, asc, NULL (sorting). Defaults NULL. round Integer; number decimal places proportions. Defaults 3. numeric Logical; TRUE, returns data frame numeric columns, base n row removed. Defaults FALSE.

title: Create a top/bottom box table or crosstab for a grid question — tab_grid_t
text: table (y = NULL) crosstab (y specified), data frame.

what: Create a table or crosstab for a multiple response (pick-any) set — tab_mr
title: Create a table or crosstab for a multiple response (pick-any) set — tab_mr
text: function produces table crosstab multiple response (pick-) set questions. x specified, output table items multiple response set. x y specified, output crosstab multiple response set y. Use weight argument produce weighted tables; omitted, results unweighted. Set prop = TRUE (default) output proportions, prop = FALSE output frequencies Use total argument add total column y specified. Ignored one-way tables. Use sort argument (desc asc) order rows.  Set sort = NULL sorting. Use round argument control decimal precision percentage tables. numeric = TRUE, function removes base row coerces values numeric, making output math-ready. Supported coding schemes multiple response items include: Unchecked / Checked Selected / Selected 0 / 1

title: Create a table or crosstab for a multiple response (pick-any) set — tab_mr
code: tab_mr(   data,   x,   y = NULL,   weight = NULL,   prop = TRUE,   total = TRUE,   sort = NULL,   round = 3,   numeric = FALSE )

title: Create a table or crosstab for a multiple response (pick-any) set — tab_mr
text: data data frame containing survey data. x variable prefix identifying multiple response set (e.g., Q2 Q2_1, Q2_2, ...). y (Optional) variable cross-tabulate multiple response set. Defaults NULL. weight (Optional) numeric weighting variable. NULL (default), results unweighted. prop Logical; TRUE (default), outputs proportions. FALSE, outputs frequencies. total Logical; TRUE (default), adds total column y specified. Ignored single-variable tables. sort Sorting order rows: desc, asc, NULL (sorting). Defaults NULL. round Integer; number decimal places proportions. Defaults 3. numeric Logical; TRUE, returns data frame numeric columns, n row removed. Defaults FALSE.

title: Create a table or crosstab for a multiple response (pick-any) set — tab_mr
text: table (y = NULL) crosstab (y specified), data frame.

what: Generate a thematic code list using the OpenAI API — theme_gpt
title: Generate a thematic code list using the OpenAI API — theme_gpt
text: function analyzes open-ended survey responses automatically generates set thematic codes descriptions. Additional custom instructions can supplied instructions argument.

title: Generate a thematic code list using the OpenAI API — theme_gpt
code: theme_gpt(   data,   x,   n = NULL,   sample = NULL,   model = "gpt-4o",   instructions = NULL )

title: Generate a thematic code list using the OpenAI API — theme_gpt
text: data data frame containing survey data. x open-ended variable analyze. n Integer; number themes return. NULL (default), model determines appropriate number themes based responses. sample Optional integer specifying number responses sample analysis. NULL, valid responses used. model Character string; OpenAI model use. Defaults gpt-4o. instructions Optional string; additional instructions coding.

title: Generate a thematic code list using the OpenAI API — theme_gpt
text: table containing generated thematic code list description. Standard codes (, None, know) included automatically.

title: Generate a thematic code list using the OpenAI API — theme_gpt
text: Requires OpenAI API key, can generated https://platform.openai.com/, set R session using Sys.setenv(OPENAI_API_KEY="..."). output tibble three columns: Code: unique numeric code theme (standard codes 97–99 added automatically). Bin: Short label theme, written sentence case. Description: one-sentence summary describing theme's content. Standard codes included automatically: 97 = 98 = None 99 = know Use function create theme_list input code_gpt(), copy paste Excel coding workbook. Note: ’s best review refine generated codes using code_gpt().

title: Generate a thematic code list using the OpenAI API — theme_gpt
code: # Generate a theme list for Q5 theme_list <- theme_gpt(data = survey_data, x = Q5) #> Error: These packages are required but not installed: dplyr, stringr
